treated differently. An accused can be convicted for 
rape only if the court reaches a conclusion that the 
intention of the accused was mala fide, and that he 
had clandestine motives.
. . .
24. Hence, it is evident that there must be adequate 
evidence to show that at the relevant time i.e. at 
the initial stage itself, the accused had no intention 
whatsoever, of keeping his promise to marry the 
victim. There may, of course, be circumstances, when a 
person having the best of intentions is unable to marry the 
victim owing to various unavoidable circumstances. The 
“failure to keep a promise made with respect to a future 
uncertain date, due to reasons that are not very clear 
from the evidence available, does not always amount to 
misconception of fact. In order to come within the meaning 
of the term “misconception of fact”, the fact must have 
an immediate relevance”. Section 90 IPC cannot be 
called into aid in such a situation, to pardon the act
